3 min read
Just now

--

While starting Java backend development, one common confusion is:
“Why learn Java 8 when Java 26 already exists?”
The answer is simple Java 8 is the foundation of modern Java. Most backend code, frameworks like Spring Boot, and interview questions are still heavily based on Java 8 concepts.
That’s why Java 8 is considered a must-know milestone, even today.
Key Feature: 1. Lambda Expressions
A Lambda Expression is an anonymous function that allows you to write more concise and readable code.
It helps in:

• Reducing boilerplate code

• Improving readability

• Enabling functional programming style in Java

• Supporting parallel processing in streams
How to write a Lambda Expression?
You can convert a normal method into a lambda in 4 simple steps:
Step 1: Remove access modifier
Step 2: Remove return type
Step 3: Remove method name
Step 4: Add -> (arrow operator)
Example
private void sayHello() {
System.out.println("Hello");
}() -> System.out.println("Hello");But where do we actually use lambda expressions?
Lambda expressions are used to invoke functional interfaces. A functional interface is simply an interface that contains only one abstract method.
Along with that, it can have any number of default and static methods, but the key rule is that there must be exactly one abstract method.
To make this clear and avoid confusion, we usually annotate it with @FunctionalInterface. This ensures that the compiler enforces the rule and prevents accidentally adding more than one abstract method.
What is a default method?
A default method is a method defined inside an interface that has a body (implementation).
Earlier, in Java versions up to Java 7, interfaces were only allowed to have abstract methods (methods without a body). But starting from Java 8, Java introduced the ability to define concrete methods inside interfaces using the default keyword.
This means interfaces can now provide a ready-to-use implementation, not just a contract.
Static method in an interface
A static method in an interface is a method that has a body and is declared using the static keyword.
Unlike default methods, static methods belong to the interface itself, not to the implementing classes.
Because of this:

• It cannot be overridden in implementing classes

• It cannot be accessed through an object or implementation class

• It can only be called using the interface name
So the question still remains: where do we actually use lambda expressions?
When an interface has only one abstract method, we don’t need to create a separate implementation class every time. Instead, we can directly use a lambda expression to provide the implementation in a clean and concise way.
In this case, the functional interface acts like a data type for the lambda expression. It defines the “shape” of the function, and the lambda provides the actual behavior.
For example:
We define a functional interface:
public interface Student {
void study();
}Now instead of creating a separate class like:
class StudentImpl implements Student {
public void study() {
System.out.println("Studying");
}
}We can directly use a lambda expression:
Student s = () -> System.out.println("Studying");
s.study();Java 8 is not just about Lambda expressions! there’s a lot more to it, like Streams, Functional Interfaces, Optional, Method References and more.
These features together introduced a more functional programming style in Java, making code cleaner, more readable, and easier to work with collections and data processing.
We’ll explore all of these step by step in the upcoming parts.
